name: Billy Butterfield
abstract: Charles William Butterfield (January 14, 1917 – March 18, 1988) was an American jazz bandleader, trumpeter, flugelhornist, and cornetist.

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Salute to Eddie Condon
Newport Jazz Festival: The Stardust Road: A Hoagy Carmichael Jubilee

about_quotes.speaker: Vince DiMartino
about_quotes.evidence: he said, “Hey Bob, you know my son’s been playing trumpet and I think he sounds pretty good, so is there any recordings that I should have, I should get him?” And we didn’t have a lot of money. So he named like four recordings and it was Miles Davis, Maynard Ferguson, Rafael Mendez and Billy Butterf
about_quotes.doc_title: Vince DiMartino interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, January 8, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Vic Dickenson
about_quotes.evidence: The transcript only contains the interviewer mentioning Billy Butterfield; the interviewee (Vic Dickenson) does not speak or reference a personal relationship with him.

about_quotes.speaker: Ralph Sutton
about_quotes.evidence: And the band was Benny Morton on trombone, Yank Lawson, Billy Butterfield, Bud Freeman, Bob Wilber, Gus Johnson on drums, Bob Haggart and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ralph Sutton interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 22, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Conniff
about_quotes.evidence: We did two albums with Billy Butterfield. One was called “Conniff Meets Butterfield,” and the other was called “Just Kiddin’ Around.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Conniff interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 14, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Jay Cee Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: Some of J. C.'s songs were recorded by the following people: ... Billy Butterfield ...

about_quotes.speaker: Artie Shaw
about_quotes.evidence: You know how I kept Billy Butterfield in the band? Billy was a very fine player... He was lead trumpet player
about_quotes.doc_title: Artie Shaw — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
